description_long: This object is a cluster of three irregularly shaped gray stones of varying sizes, with the largest stone standing upright and the others leaning or resting against it. The surfaces are faceted and angular, featuring visible cracks and subtle white veining that suggest natural fracturing. Patches of green moss or lichen are scattered across the lower portions, indicating some degree of weathering and exposure to moisture. The stones have a matte, slightly rough finish, with no polished or glossy areas. The overall form is blocky and solid, with no hollow or recessed sections, making it a substantial obstacle. The coloration is predominantly gray with hints of green and white, giving it a natural, urban landscaping appearance.
description: A cluster of three gray, angular stones with cracks, white veining, and patches of green moss, forming a solid, weathered barrier.
description_view_0: The front view shows the largest stone upright with two smaller stones clustered at its base, highlighting the cracks and green moss on their surfaces.
description_view_1: This side view emphasizes the tall, narrow profile of the largest stone and the angular arrangement of the group, with visible veining and moss.
description_view_2: The rear view reveals the flat, less detailed backs of the stones, with more muted green patches and fewer visible cracks.
description_view_3: The top-down view displays the broad, flat surfaces and the relative size differences between the stones, with cracks and moss visible on the largest stone.
attribute_placement: set as a standalone feature or grouped with other stones on the ground
attribute_pattern: Irregular white veining and cracks, with mottled green patches of moss or lichen; the pattern is natural and non-repetitive.
attribute_weathering: Surfaces are cracked and slightly rough with visible moss, indicating moderate weathering and exposure to outdoor elements.
attribute_integration_with_surroundings: Partially blends with natural landscaping elements, with moss suggesting some integration with soil and vegetation, but stands out against paved or built environments.
